new_value: A 7-ADD whirl compound: ducking, paradox, symposium, and spinning all layered onto the whirl base in a single continuous rotation.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value:
Begin from a clipper set with the supporting leg planted under the bag. The whirl rotation carries the body around the planted leg while the body simultaneously holds the symposium body line, the paradox same-side reset, and the ducking head-low compression. The body-spin (spinning) completes the figure as the bag returns to the clipper position.

The four modifiers are composed onto the whirl frame, not sequenced. Difficulty comes from holding all four body positions through one continuous rotation rather than chaining them.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value: Most practitioners reach montage after each of its constituents (ducking-whirl, paradox-whirl, symposium-whirl, spinning-whirl) is clean in isolation. The symposium body line combined with the ducking head-low compression is the load-bearing junction; if either drifts, the spinning rotation tends to flatten and the paradox reset is lost. Practice the body posture before adding rotation.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value: Confident execution of the 4-ADD whirl compounds (ducking-whirl, paradox-whirl, symposium-whirl) is the natural prerequisite. Spinning-whirl at 5 ADD introduces the body-spin component that montage rides on top of.

new_value: A 5-ADD butterfly compound: nuclear's paradox-atomic +2 weighting layered onto the butterfly base.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value:
From a clipper set, body opens with a same-side out kick carrying both dex and paradox direction. Body completes with an opposite-side out kick and lands cross-body in a clipper delay. The nuclear modifier is what makes this a 5-ADD trick rather than a 4-ADD same-out-then-op-out butterfly: nuclear's +2 ADD comes from the paradox-atomic body weighting that the same-out kick carries explicitly.

Operationally the trick is four steps: clipper start, same-out (dex)(pdx), op-out (dex), op-clip cross-body delay.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value: Nuclear's +2 ADD weighting reflects the paradox-atomic body commitment. The most common miss is the same-out paradox: practitioners reach for the kick without committing the body direction first. The paradox marker in the operational notation is where the +2 anchors -- ensure body weight is committed before the dex.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value: Comfortable execution of any 4-ADD butterfly compound is the natural entry point. Atomic-butterfly (4 ADD) shares the most body mechanics with Matador. Nuclear's +2 ADD weight (the paradox-atomic body commitment) is what carries the trick from 4 to 5 ADD.

new_value: A 6-ADD blender compound: ducking and paradox layered onto the Whirling-Osis rotational base.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value:
From a clipper set, drop into a ducking body compression, then execute a same-side front whirl with both dex and paradox direction. Body reverses to a back-facing spin, then lands cross-body in a same-side clipper delay. Five operational steps: clip, duck, same-front-whirl (dex)(pdx), (back)-spin (bod), same-clip cross-body.

The blender base (transitively Whirling Osis) carries 4 ADD; ducking + paradox add 2 more. The (front) and (back) annotations in the operational notation track the body orientation across the spin transition -- this is the load-bearing rotational signature of the blender family.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value: Pairs naturally with Spender (Spinning + Paradox + Blender; also 6 ADD). Both are 6-ADD blender compounds; both carry paradox; the difference is body-modifier: Spender carries spinning (body rotation), Mind Bender carries ducking (body compression). Compare the two side-by-side for cross-modifier feel.
source_note: UX3b1 schema-backed prose backfill 2026-05-11: migrate from UX2_PILOT_RAW service-layer constant into freestyle_tricks columns.

new_value: Comfortable execution of blender (4 ADD) and paradox-blender (5 ADD) is the natural entry point. The ducking modifier should be solid on a separate base (ducking-whirl, ducking-butterfly) before attempting Mind Bender. Spender is the natural same-tier sibling to study alongside.

new_value: Orbit circles the footbag once between two toe delays, the same movement as around the world run in the opposite direction. From a toe delay, set the bag straight up, wait until it has left your foot, then circle the leg around it and recatch on the same toe. Around the world and orbit are direction companions, and both are worth the same number of adds.
source_note: Editorial: authored a real About modeled on around the world with the direction reversed; replaces the stub description so the foundation card can link.

new_value: Orbit circles the bag once between two toe delays in the reverse direction from around the world. From a toe delay, set the bag straight up to about knee height, wait until it has clearly left your foot, then circle the leg around it the opposite way and catch it again on the same toe. The circle itself is small and unhurried; the work is in the set and the recatch, not in racing the leg around.
source_note: Editorial: authored from the around-the-world technique with the direction reversed.

new_value: Think of it in three parts, set, dexterity, catch, and keep them separate so the leg does not collide with the bag by circling too early. Wait until the bag has clearly left the foot before you start the circle. If you already have around the world, orbit is its reverse-direction counterpart, so practice the reverse circle on both legs.

new_value: A cross-body inside-foot stall performed on the support side. Clipper is one of the core foundations of freestyle footbag and serves as the basis for many advanced tricks.
source_note: Curator-authored description; clipper is a stall

new_value: Pass the support leg over the footbag and catch on a delay. For example, from a left toe stall, hop off the right leg and bring it over the top of the footbag and catch it on a right toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org /newmoves.

new_value: From a clipper delay set, bring the setting leg straight from the clipper around the footbag from out to in twice. Hop off the support leg as you bring the setting leg down to a clipper delay with the support leg.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org /newmoves.

new_value: Basically a double swirl. Set the bag on a right clipper; while in the cross-body position, circle the footbag twice (in either direction) and catch it on the same-side clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org /newmoves.

new_value: Start by inside delaying the footbag. Then, while wrapping the footbag, complete a 360-degree spin towards the support leg. Eliminate any pauses between the initial inside delay and the spin to keep the move smooth.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org /newmoves.

new_value: A blender, but the dexterity circles in the other direction.
source_note: Adapted from footbag.org /newmoves.

new_value: Same as double over down except set from one leg and circle with the other. For example, from a right toe delay, set the bag across slightly and circle the bag twice from out to in with your left leg; hop off the right leg as you bring the left leg down and catch on a right-leg clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org /newmoves.

new_value: A 2-ADD swing element.

new_value: A 2-ADD swing dexterity.

old_value: A 2-ADD flying body-kick; also called the dragonfly.
new_value: Pass a leg over the footbag from in to out then jump and kick the footbag with the inside surface of the support leg. For example, bring the left leg over the footbag from in to out. Now jump off the right leg and kick the footbag with the inside surface of the right foot and land on this leg.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Delay the footbag on the toes on its way down to the ground and continue the downward motion keeping the leg perfectly straight. Keeping a smooth motion swing your leg back and bend at the knee flicking the footbag over your head to the ground in front of you. The key to the pendulum is smooth acceleration while keeping your leg straight as long as physically possible.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Catch the footbag in the crook of the leg. It is easiest to point the knee straight down and bend the support leg slightly for balance. Wait until the bag hits the back of the knee before squeezing it.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Blind opposite-shoulder clipper stall (fborg-2add): the bag passes behind the head. 2 ADD = clipper-stall ([XBD] + [DEL]).
new_value: This is a clipper stall set from the opposite side shoulder. The footbag must pass behind the head- ie. blind.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Opposite of a pendulum. Toe delay the footbag behind you (actually, more to your side), and scoop the footbag, swinging your leg forward and across your front.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Jump and kick the bag with the toes with the kicking leg tucked behind (fborg-2add). 2 ADD = [BOD] + [XBD]; kick, no terminal stall.
new_value: Jump and kick the footbag with the toes with the kicking leg tucked behind the jumping leg.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A leg over straight after a mirage. The mirage can be set either from a clipper or a toe delay. From the set hop off the support leg then the setting leg and pass both over the footbag and finally catch the footbag with the setting foot on a toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Basically an in to out double around the world set from a clipper delay. For example, from a left clipper delay, set the bag just above waist height. Quickly plant the left leg and circle the footbag from in to out twice landing on right toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A miraging clipper delay. From a clipper- quickly plant the setting leg and bring the support leg over the bag from in to out and in one motion- keeping this leg close to the other leg- into a clipper delay on the other side. Or, setting from left toe, quickly plant the left leg and bring the right leg up and around the bag from in to out. Turn slightly in the direction of the dexterity (in this case, to the right) to help bring the right foot back around into the cross body position to end the move on a right foot clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Three consecutive 360-degree body spins between kicks. 3 ADD = three spins.
new_value: Complete three 360 degree spins inbetween kicks.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Double fairy: two outside dexes into an opposite toe stall. 3 ADD = dex(2) + toe-stall(1). Double-the-dex with set-implies-toe-stall.
new_value: Set from right toe. Quickly circle the bag from out to in twice. End the move by catching the bag on a left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: from a left toe set, quickly circle the bag from out to in with the left foot. End the move by catching the bag on a left foot clipper.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Pixie modifier with opposite-side clipper terminator. 3 ADD = pixie(+1) + clipper-stall(2); JOB tokens [DEX] + [XBD] + [DEL].
new_value: From a left toe set, perform a quick in to out dexterity with the setting leg. End by catching the bag on a right foot clipper.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Pixie modifier with same-side clipper terminator. 3 ADD = pixie(+1) + clipper-stall(2); JOB tokens [DEX] + [XBD] + [DEL].
new_value: From a left toe set, perform a quick in to out dexterity with the setting leg. End by catching the bag on a left foot clipper.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: From a right toe, perform a quick in to out dexterity (pixie) and touch the ground with your right foot. Then, come back around the bag from out to in ending on a left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A whirling osis. Begin like a whirl but then bail to an osis. For example, From a right clipper delay- whirl over the footbag with the left leg but don't put it down- instead turn out of the move scooping the bag into a left osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Two consecutive full leg circles ending in a heel delay (terminal-surface variant of double-around-the-world; heel carries [UNS] unusual-surface tag).
new_value: A double around the world ending in a heel delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Doubled out-to-in dex from a toe set, ending in opposite-leg clipper delay. 4 ADD = [DEX] + [DEX] + [XBD] + [DEL]. FB.org-confirmed JOB.
new_value: From a toe delay circle the footbag from out to in twice and catch the footbag on the opposite leg clipper. For example, From a right toe delay- set the bag straight up and circle the bag twice from out to in- hop off the left leg as your bring the right leg down and catch on a left leg clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Two out-to-in dexterities between two same-side clipper delays (clipper-set entry variant of double-over-down). 4 ADD = [DEX] + [DEX] + [XBD] + [DEL]. FB.org-confirmed JOB.
new_value: Two out-to-in dexterities in between 2 same-side clipper delays. For example, Right clipper delay- set the bag straight up and circle it twice from out to in- then delay the footbag on your right clipper again.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A mirage into an eclipse. For example, Set the footbag from a right clipper and do an in to out dexterity with your left leg. Then jump and straight after initiate an inside delay. Bring the jumping leg over the delaying leg and back down to the ground.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: For example, From a right foot clipper delay- circle the bag with the right foot from in to out twice then end on a left foot toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Paradox dlo (FB.org: paradox mirage with extra legover at the end).
new_value: For example, Set from a left foot clipper delay- do a left leg in to out dexterity- then a right leg out to in dexterity and catch the footbag on a right toe delay. This is a paradox mirage with an extra legover at the end.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: For example, Set the footbag from your right foot clipper and switch your support leg (raise your left leg as you plant the right foot). Then, without planting the left foot, hop off of the right leg bringing it up and over the bag from in to out ending on a left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A whirl delay that is set from one clipper delay and caught on the other. Set the bag so it moves across the front of your body and bring the setting leg from behind the support leg- and without putting this leg down- begin the whirl motion and end on a clipper using the other leg to do the delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Symposium modifier on double-leg-over base. 4 ADD = symposium(+1) + double-leg-over(3); JOB SET > (no plant while) OP IN [BOD] [DEX] > OP OUT [DEX] > SAME TOE [DEL]. Symposium no-plant chassis: [BOD] injected into DLO's first IN-dex; second OUT-dex preserved.
new_value: For example, From a right toe delay- jump with both legs going over the footbag and ending on another right toe delay. Note make sure both legs go over the footbag and you do not jump around the bag.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Identical to the symposium whirl delay but you circle the footbag the other way. For example, From a right clipper delay jump hard off the left leg and bring it over the footbag from the back up and over without putting the right leg down. Land back on the left leg. The dexterity motion comes from the knee (as in a whirl), not from the hip (as in butterfly).
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Identical to the whirl delay but you never put the setting leg down. For example, From a right clipper delay jump hard off the left leg and bring it over the footbag from the front up and over without putting the right leg down. Land back on the left leg.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A whirl delay followed by a swirl. For example, From a right clipper delay plant the right leg and bring the left leg over the bag from the front up and around to land this leg back in its original clipper position. After this leg has circled the bag and is racing to the ground hop off the right leg and complete an inside around the world before using this leg to finish on a clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Double whirl: two inside dexes into a clipper stall. 4 ADD = dex(2) + clipper-stall(2). Multiplicity of whirl.
new_value: A double whirl. For example, Right clipper set- circle the bag twice in the whirling direction- back to a right clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: For example, Right clipper delay- duck the bag- right dexterity from out to in to a left clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Set the footbag just over your head. Duck under the bag. Complete the move with either osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: For example, From a right clipper- let the bag pass behind your back and without putting the right leg down turn to the right and complete a butterfly delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Pixie modifier on swirl base. 4 ADD = pixie(+1) + swirl(3); JOB TOE > SAME IN [DEX] > SAME/OP BACK SWIRL [DEX] > SAME CLIP [XBD] [DEL]. FB.org-confirmed.
new_value: For example, Right toe set- circle the footbag with your right leg from in to out- hop to the right and do a right leg swirl- coming from the back- up and over the bag- landing on a right clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Spin before catching the footbag on an Osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A stepping mirage with a duck between the two dexes.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: This move is basically a blender with a plant immediately following the dex.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Basically a blurry double switch. For example, From a right clipper delay- quickly plant the right leg and bring the left leg over the bag as you hop off the right leg. Bring the right leg around the bag from out to in twice ending on a right toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A stepping set into a reverse whirl on the opposite side of your body.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Double-spinning compound on osis base. 5 ADD = double-spinning(+2) + osis(3); JOB CLIP > (back) SPIN [BOD] > (back) SPIN [BOD] > (back) SPIN [BOD] > OP CLIP [XBD] [DEL]. Triple back-spin compound.
new_value: Two complete 360 degree spins before an osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Double-over-down chassis ending in an opposite-leg back swirl + same-side clipper terminator. 5 ADD = [DEX] + [DEX] + [DEX] + [XBD] + [DEL]. FB.org-confirmed.
new_value: A Double-Over Down ending in a swirl. For example, Right toe set- circle the footbag from out to in with your right leg twice- then perform a left leg swirl ending in a left leg clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Two out-to-in dexterities from a toe set ending on a cross-body sole (flapper) delay. 5 ADD = [DEX] + [DEX] + [XBD] + [UNS] + [DEL]; JOB TOE > OP OUT [DEX] > SAME OUT [DEX] > OP SOLE [XBD] [UNS] [DEL]. FB.org-confirmed.
new_value: For example, From a right toe set- hop off the left leg and circle the bag twice from out to in and end on a right flapper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A blurry reverse symposium mirage. For example, Right clipper set- quick blurry- jump over the bag with the right leg and complete a out to in dexterity without planting the left foot. End in left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: This is a blurry symposium paradox mirage. For example, Set this move from a Right clipper delay. Set the bag across your body and do a left leg mirage. Now hop hard off your right leg and do a right leg symposium in to out mirage- catching the footbag in a left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A barrage into an osis. For example, From a right foot clipper delay quickly plant the right foot and hop off the left leg bringing it around the footbag from in to out twice. As you complete the second dexterity hop off the right foot and turn to the left catching the footbag in a right foot osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A stepping opposite side barfly For example, From a right foot clipper- quickly plant the right foot and bring the left leg over the bag from in to out. Then as you quickly plant the left leg- hop off the right foot and bring it around the bag from out to in twice. Then end on a left foot clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A furious barfly. For example, From a right clipper set- circle the bag from in to out twice with the left leg then circle twice with the right leg to a left clipper delay.
source_note: footbag.org corpus text; Nemesis is ruled a Furious Barfly.

new_value: A mirage into an osis. For example, from a right clipper delay, quickly plant the right leg and bring your left leg over the bag, simultaneously hopping off the right leg as you spin to the left to catch the bag on a right foot osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Circle the bag clockwise with the foot before a toe stall.
new_value: Circle one leg all the way around the footbag between two toe delays. Around the world is the inward circle; the outward, reverse circle is the orbit.
source_note: Around the world is the inward circle only; the outward reverse circle is the separate orbit atom.

new_value: A hop-over performed in the air. Jump and immediately initiate an inside delay. Bring the jumping leg over the delaying leg and back down to the ground, ending in a clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus (typo fixed).

new_value: A double dexterity legover. For example, from a left toe delay, hop off the right leg and circle the footbag from out to in twice, ending on a right toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Spin into a clipper delay. Set it from a clipper stall on the other foot, or from a toe delay with the foot straight in front of you; turn so the footbag passes behind your back and catch it on a clipper delay.
source_note: Adapted (trimmed) from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A two-dex compound from a clipper stall: from a right clipper delay, quickly plant the right clipper and circle the bag from in to out twice with the left leg, then delay on the left clipper.
source_note: Adapted from footbag.org corpus (lead sentence added).

new_value: A mirage into a butterfly delay. For example, from a right clipper delay, bring the left leg over the footbag by turning your hips to the left. Without putting the left down, hop off the right leg and simultaneously bring this leg over the footbag from out to in and catch the footbag in a left clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus (decomposition to be reconciled separately; description documents the movement).

old_value: Rotational body-spin dex; anchor of the whirl family.
new_value: From either a clipper or a toe delay, set the footbag up, then with the support leg circle the footbag from the front up and over the footbag. Hop off the original setting leg as you finish the dexterity and catch the bag on a clipper delay. It is easiest to point your toes down as you circle the bag, because this makes it faster and less likely to snag the bag.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus (forward whirl).

new_value: A whirl with the direction of the dexterity reversed. As in a whirl, the dexterity is performed with the knee, with the ankle circling the bag. This is unlike a butterfly, where the hips are used to make the upper leg do the dexterity.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus (reverse whirl; disambiguated from the forward whirl entry).

old_value: Reverse-direction rotational dex; anchor of the swirl family.
new_value: Cross-body inside around the world. The back dexterity is performed by bringing the foot around the bag from the back, then up and over the top.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus (forward swirl).

old_value: A swirl performed in the reverse direction (3 ADD).
new_value: Same as a swirl except the direction of the dexterity is reversed.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus (reverse swirl).

old_value: Barfly with an added back-swirl dex.
new_value: For example, From a right clipper set the bag around waist height travelling across your body. Circle the bag from out to in twice with the right leg. Then hop off the left leg and swirl the footbag with this leg ending in left clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: A blurry reverse symposium mirage. For example, Right clipper set- quick blurry- jump over the bag with the right leg and complete a out to in dexterity without planting the left foot. End in left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Butterfly with an added back-swirl dex.
new_value: Same as butterfly delay except instead of just catching the footbag on a clipper- complete a cross body inside around the world (swirl) delay straight after the leg over.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Circle the footbag twice between toe delays. Pointing your toes down will prevent the circling leg from snagging the footbag as you come around for the second time.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Jumping body kick into clipper position.
new_value: Jump and kick the footbag with the inside surface of the foot with the kicking leg tucked behind the jumping leg. It's easiest to land on the same leg that kicks the bag. This move is also called "Jester".
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Jump and kick the footbag with the inside surface of the foot.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Jump and kick the footbag with the outside surface of the foot.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Balance the bag on top of your head.
new_value: Delay the footbag on the top of the head. Look slightly upwards to the get the top of the head perfectly flat.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Catch and balance the bag on your heel.
new_value: Delay the footbag on the heel of the foot. Point toes at their max to create the easiest stalling surface between the calf and the heel.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Hold an inside delay close to the ground and hop over it with the support leg.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Paradon with an added back-swirl dex.
new_value: A Paradon ending in a swirl. For example, Right toe set- circle the footbag from out to in with your left leg twice- then perform a right leg swirl ending in a right leg clipper delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: For example, From a left foot clipper stall- bring the left leg around the support leg and around the bag from in to out completing the 'S' shape. Then hop off your right leg and bring it around the bag from in to out landing a left toe delay.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Balance the bag on your shoulder.
new_value: Delay the footbag on the shoulder. Use your cheek at first to help to catch the footbag.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Catch and balance the bag on the sole of your raised foot.
new_value: Delay the footbag on the sole of the foot. To ensure a flat level landing surface- it is easiest to point the knee straight down and bend the support leg slightly for balance.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

old_value: Folk name; decomposes to reverse swirling osis.
new_value: A swirling osis where the swirl is done with the opposite leg as the osis. Similar to a dyno except the swirl is done with the other foot. For example, From a right clipper delay complete an inside cross body around the world. Just as your leg is heading back down to the ground- hop off the left leg- turn out of the move scooping the bag into a left osis.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Put an inside delay on the ground and step over it with the opposite leg.
source_note: Imported from footbag.org corpus.

new_value: Mirage is the foundational dex: the opposite leg dexes around the supporting leg while the bag returns to the toe that set it. From a toe delay, set the bag straight up to about waist height and plant the setting foot quickly. Hop off the planted leg and bring the dexterity leg up and over the bag from inside to outside, keeping your weight forward over the bag, then catch the bag back on the setting toe as it drops; the dexterity leg clears and plants without making the catch. Think of carrying the leg over the bag rather than swinging it around.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Set the bag straight up, not arced. A straight set lets the dex leg clear it at the peak; an arc forces you to chase the bag and costs energy. The most common early miss is starting the dex before the bag has fully left the setting foot, which carries the bag around the support leg instead of sending it straight up. Start with a low set, around knee height, so the support leg has time to complete the dex and land, then raise the set as leg speed improves. Turning the body slightly toward the dexterity makes the leg clear more easily.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Repeatable toe delays on both feet are the foundation. Drill alternating left and right toe delays before adding the dex. Because the move depends on hopping high enough to carry the leg over the bag, leg-jump strength is the limiting factor for many learners.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Drifter is a mirage that finishes on a clipper instead of a toe. From a clipper set, plant the setting leg and bring the support leg over the bag from inside to outside in one motion, turning slightly toward the dexterity to carry the foot back into the cross-body position. Keep the dexterity leg close to the other leg and land on a clipper (cross-body) delay on the far side. The cross-body clipper catch is what carries it from the mirage's 2 ADD to 3.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The simplest way in is to do it exactly like a clipper-set mirage, set a touch higher, and catch on a clipper rather than a toe; no stepping is needed. Clear the dex when the bag is at the top of its set, and time your jump so you land just as the bag arrives. A small hop or slide of the support leg toward the bag during the dex helps you land cross-body on the far side. Done as one fluid motion this is a true drifter; splitting it into a stepping set followed by a separate clipper is a different, rougher version some call a crispy drifter.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable mirage and a solid clipper (cross-body) delay are the building blocks, since the drifter combines a mirage-style dex with a clipper catch. Comfort setting and catching on both sides helps, because the finish crosses the body.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Osis is a full spin into a clipper delay. Set the bag straight up to about waist height, ideally from a clipper or from a toe delay with the foot in front of you, then turn so the bag passes behind your back. Keep your eyes on the bag as long as you can before committing to the spin, then turn quickly and catch it on the inside (clipper) surface of your foot, bending the support leg to cushion the catch. The body spin plus the cross-body clipper catch are the trick's 3 ADD.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Set almost as if the bag is going into your back pocket, and keep your eyes on it until just before it lands. Bend the support leg as you catch to absorb the delay, and get the stalling foot as flat as possible so the bag settles rather than rolling off. A strong drill is learning to stop the bag dead on the clipper instead of flicking it straight back out; flicking is easier, but stopping it cleanly is the real mark of control. Many learners find it by starting as if they will follow through, then stopping at the last second to find the catch position.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dependable clipper (cross-body) delay is essential, since the spin lands on one. Most learn osis set from an existing clipper or from a front toe delay rather than from behind the body. Spotting the bag through the turn is the genuinely new skill the spin adds; if you can already catch the bag back there without spinning, the move is mostly about adding the rotation.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clipper is a cross-body inside-foot stall: the catching foot settles the bag on its inside surface, tucked behind and across the support leg. Step forward onto the support leg and bend it deeply, well past what feels necessary, then bring the catching foot in behind the support knee and down with the bag, catching it on a flat inside surface low and close to the support leg. Stay over the bag with your shoulders open and your eyes down on it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Bending the support leg deeply is the whole move; cushion the bag by bending the support leg rather than by lowering the catching foot. Get the support knee close to a right angle, even if you have to hop slightly into the bend to reach it. Keep the bag low and directly underneath you, not behind you, which is the most common early mistake. A flat catching surface holds the bag, and standing briefly on the side of the foot teaches how far the ankle has to bend. Practicing the position without a bag, or balancing a book on the foot in the clipper position, builds the balance the stall needs.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Learn the inside stall first; a clipper is an inside stall taken behind and across the support leg, and players who can already hold an inside stall pick it up quickly. The toe delay and inside delay are the natural moves to have before this one. Clipper is itself a foundation for many harder tricks, so time spent making it consistent pays off later.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A toe delay catches and balances the bag on the flat top of the foot. Follow the bag up with your foot, then bring the foot down with the bag at the same speed it is falling, cushioning it to a stop rather than letting it bounce. Keep the support knee slightly bent and your balance settled over one foot, and let the bag make a small dent in the surface so it stays put.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The one thing nearly every learner misses is lowering the foot as the bag lands; bring your foot down with the bag, matching its speed, instead of holding the foot still and hoping the bag stops. Think of it as catching an egg, or a cricket ball, absorbing the fall rather than meeting it. A slight downward dip at the moment of contact is what kills the bounce. Learning barefoot first lets you feel the bag settle, since the top of the foot forms a natural pocket.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: This is usually the first delay a player learns, so the only real prerequisite is steady balance on one foot while the other lifts up and down. Everything past that is timing, which comes with practice.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Butterfly reaches a clipper delay by stepping the support leg over the bag rather than planting it across, so the legs finish in the clipper position but arrive there over the top of the bag. Set the bag to about thigh height, stay over it looking straight down through it, and carry the support leg out and over using the hips and thigh rather than the shin and ankle. Hop off the leg that will make the clipper so you land as the bag arrives, catching it on a cross-body clipper.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It helps to think of butterfly as a clipper you step over the bag into, since the catch and the finishing position are the same. Keep the set low, around thigh height; popping it too high makes the timing harder. Drive the dexterity from the hips and thigh, not the lower leg, and land on the ball of the foot rather than flat. Hopping off the foot that will clip lets you come down exactly as the bag does.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid clipper delay is the foundation, since butterfly finishes on one and shares its catching position. Comfort with the cross-body clipper on both sides makes the over-the-top dexterity much easier to time.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Whirl circles the support leg around the bag from the front, up and over, and lands on a clipper delay. Set the bag from a clipper or toe delay to just above knee height, then hop off the setting leg and bring the support leg over the top, pointing the upper leg and knee straight down and circling with the lower leg and ankle. Point the toes down through the circle so the leg clears quickly without snagging, and catch on a cross-body clipper.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keep the upper leg and knee pointed straight down and move only the lower leg; letting the thigh swing turns it into a butterfly-like motion instead of a whirl. The hop off the setting leg as you set is what makes the dexterity fast enough to come around in time. Circling with the ankle rather than the calf opens a wider window to clear the bag. Point the toes down throughout so the leg does not catch the bag.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Make the clipper solid first; you should be able to hold at least three controlled consecutive clippers before adding the whirl, since the move sets from and lands on one. Working both legs from the start keeps the trick from becoming one-sided.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Legover passes the opposite leg over the top of the bag and catches the bag on that same leg. From a toe set, hop off the setting leg before it lands and bring the opposite leg over the bag from outside to inside in one smooth, quick motion, then catch on the toe of that dexing leg, not the setting foot. The hop and the set happen together, which buys the time to clear the leg over the bag.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Treat it as one smooth, quick motion rather than separate steps; flick the set straight up and keep your eye on the bag as the leg comes over. Hopping with the setting leg as you set is the key timing trick, since the dexterity is far easier to clear while you are already in the air. A belly-height set gives enough time to bring the leg around comfortably.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable toe delay to set from and catch on is the foundation. Players who can set with a pixie often find the leg comes around more easily, since the bag can be cleared while it is still rising; comfort with the illusion and other outside dexes tends to carry over to the legover.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Illusion is the outside-to-inside counterpart of the mirage: the opposite leg crosses the bag from outside back across to the inside, and the bag returns to the setting toe. Set the bag to about waist height, turn toward it, and carry the opposite leg out and then back across the bag from outside to inside, planting the setting foot as the leg comes around, then catch the bag back on the setting toe as it drops. The dexing leg clears without making the catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Two timings both work and players disagree on which is easier: starting the dexterity as you begin the set, or waiting until the bag is on its way back down before crossing under it. Waiting for the descent cuts the work and sets up the finishing delay well, so it is a good place to start. Beginning the dex early, then planting the setting foot as you come around, leaves plenty of time to hop up into the toe catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dependable toe delay to set from and catch on is the foundation. Illusion is the out-to-in mirror of the mirage and a natural companion to the legover; comfort with one outside dexterity tends to unlock the others.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: An inside delay catches and balances the bag on the instep, the inside surface of the foot. Raise the stalling foot across the body so the instep turns up toward you, the leg bent into a rough figure-four, then as the bag descends lower the foot a little slower than it is falling so the instep cushions it to a stop. Bend the support knee and keep your balance settled over the standing leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It is mostly balance: bend the support leg as the bag lands and let the instep give a little rather than meeting the bag hard. Curling the toes down helps form a pocket that holds the bag, the same habit that later helps the clipper. Some flexibility helps, so practice raising the leg to mid-body and holding the bent foot there, then drop the bag onto it a few times to learn the contact point before trying to keep it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Steady one-footed balance is the real prerequisite, the same as the toe delay. The inside stall is worth getting solid early because it is the direct foundation for the clipper, which is the same catch taken behind and across the support leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Around the world circles the bag once between two toe delays, with the foot travelling inward around the bag; the outward, reverse circle is a separate move, the orbit. From a toe delay, set the bag straight up to about knee height, wait until it has clearly left your foot, then circle the foot inward around it by bringing the knee up, and catch it again on the same toe. The circle itself is small and unhurried; the work is in the set and the recatch, not in racing the leg around.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Think of it in three parts, set, dexterity, catch, and keep them separate so the foot does not collide with the bag by circling too early. Wait until the bag has definitely left the foot before you start the circle. The set should be straight up and not too high, around knee height. Learn it on both legs from the start, and practise the outward orbit alongside it as its own move; working only one side quietly limits which later moves you can do.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Two controlled toe delays in a row, with a vertical, well-placed set between them, is the real foundation; once those are reliable, only the circling dexterity is new. Players who already have the around-the-world kick often pick it up quickly by simply stalling after the circle instead of kicking.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Pendulum delays the bag on the toes as it falls, then carries that downward motion into a smooth backward swing of a straight leg, bending at the knee at the end to flick the bag up over your head and down in front of you. Point the toes up to hold the bag on the foot, keep the leg straight as long as you physically can, and accelerate smoothly through the swing; centrifugal force is what keeps the bag on your foot.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Smooth acceleration is everything, so do not jerk the leg back. Keep the leg straight far longer than feels natural, then bend the knee only at the end to throw the bag. You do not need to watch the bag, and turning your head to look behind tends to break the move, so trust the feel. Most players find it much easier barefoot or in socks at first, since the toes can curl to hold the bag, and it is the kind of trick that clicks the first time you hit it and then repeats.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid toe delay is the starting point; from there, practice swinging the leg back and forth keeping the bag on the toe before adding the throw. It rewards patience over force, so it is worth not rushing to the flick until the swing itself feels secure.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Double leg over is a leg over taken straight after a mirage, so both legs pass over the bag in turn. Set the bag, from a clipper or a toe delay, to about waist height and close to your body, then hop off the support leg and immediately off the setting leg, passing both over the bag, and catch it on the setting foot in a toe delay. Planting the setting foot as fast as possible after the set is what makes the second pass possible.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Get the setting foot back to the ground as quickly as you can; the whole move depends on that fast plant to set up the second leg. Keep the set close to the body, about waist high, so a small turn places the bag just outside the opposite thigh where the second leg can clear it. Some players find a lower, knee-height set easier, and many learn a crispy version first, separating the stepping set from the leg over before blending them into one motion.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable mirage and a single leg over are the two pieces this combines, so both should be comfortable first. A dry drill helps: stand astride a low, stable object and practice jumping both feet across it to either side, which trains the two-leg pass without the bag.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Dragonfly kick passes one leg over the bag from inside to outside, then jumps off the other leg and kicks the bag with the inside surface of that support foot, landing on the same leg. Letting the bag drop fairly low before starting gives more room to swing the leg around. Many find it cleanest as a flyer: jump first, pass the leg over, and kick the bag with the inside of the foot all before landing.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Treat the leg-over as a familiar inside dexterity, the same pass you would make over the bag, then add the jumping inside kick. Letting the bag get close to the ground opens up room for the swing. Doing the whole thing airborne, jump then dex then inside-kick before touching down, tends to feel smoother than doing the dex on the ground and then jump-kicking. A gentle lob from a partner is a good way to drill the timing of getting the leg around and still kicking the bag back up.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort with an inside-to-outside dexterity off a toe delay is the foundation; some learn it by first doing toe stalls with an in-to-out dex between them, then adding the jumping inside kick. Being able to leave the ground and act while airborne is the new element the kick adds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Pixie sets from a toe delay, then passes the support leg up and over the bag from inside to outside, catching on the opposite toe, while the other foot stays off the ground after the set. From a toe set, plant the setting foot and a little to the side, keep that knee bent, and whip the leg around the bag, doing the dexterity with the knee rather than the foot. Sending the bag high out of the dexterity is useful, because a pixie doubles as a set for many other moves.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Drive the dexterity from the knee, not the foot; leaning over the bag and circling the leg around it keeps the motion compact. Setting from low, under the knee, gives more time and speed. Aim to send the bag high after the dexterity, which is what lets a pixie be used as the entry set for harder tricks, and gives you room to add a second dexterity later.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A consistent around the world is the natural lead-in; players who can circle the bag between toe delays usually pick up the pixie quickly, since it is the same idea with the leg passing over rather than the foot circling under. It is closely related to the symposium mirage.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A heel delay balances the bag on the heel, on the flat surface between the calf and the heel. Point the toes down at their fullest to open that surface up, set the bag just high enough to slip the heel under it, and as it lands bring the heel down slightly to cushion the impact while bending the support leg. An inside heel position, where you can see the heel better, is usually easier than an outside one.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keep the toe pointed as far down as you can; that is what makes the heel a level stalling surface. Do not set too high, or the bag bounces off the heel. A common way in is from a toe stall: pop the bag up and slightly back with the whole leg, only about eight inches, just enough to drift the heel under it. Absorb the landing the same way as a toe stall, lowering the heel a touch as the bag arrives.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid toe stall is the usual starting point, since many learn the heel delay by popping up out of a toe stall and dropping the heel under the bag. Comfort cushioning a stall by bending the support leg carries straight over.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A sole delay balances the bag on the sole of the foot, with the foot behind you. Point the knee straight down so the sole sits level, bend the support leg slightly for balance, and bring the foot down with the bag to settle it. It asks more balance and stability than the front stalls, since the catching surface is behind your body.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keeping the knee pointed straight down is the key, because that is what makes the sole level enough to hold the bag. It helps to set the bag behind you with your body slightly turned so you can watch the catch, and to hold the position for a few seconds without the bag first so the ankle learns how it has to bend. Many find it easiest to arrive at the sole from a heel delay, popping the bag straight up and rolling onto the sole.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Balance is the main demand, so the front stalls and the heel delay are good to have first, and some learn it from the heel delay directly. Comfort with a flapper, which works the same backward-foot position, makes the sole delay easier to find.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: An outside delay balances the bag on the outside edge of the foot, which is a wide, flat stalling surface. Hold the foot out to the side, catch the bag at about waist level or a little below, and cushion it by bending the support leg and letting your whole body descend rather than dropping the catching foot on its own. Extending the support leg again lifts the bag back up.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The main hurdle is the flexibility to hold the foot turned out to the side; the surface itself is forgiving. Cushion with the support leg, not the catching foot, since trying to receive the bag by lowering the delay foot tends to let it roll off the side. Letting the whole body sink as the bag lands keeps your balance and sets up an easy lift back out.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: This is one of the more approachable delays once the toe and inside stalls are comfortable; the cushion-with-the-support-leg habit from those carries directly over. Some range of motion in the ankle and hip to hold the foot out to the side is all the move really asks.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A knee delay balances the bag on top of the knee. Set the bag straight up, no higher than chest height, and bring the stalling knee up under it, straightening the leg so the top of the knee is as flat as possible. Bring the knee down slightly as the bag makes contact to cushion it, and bend the other leg to stay balanced.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A very straight, controlled set is what makes this work, since the knee is a small surface; keep the set low, around chest height. Straighten the stalling knee as much as you can to flatten the surface, and cushion by dropping the knee a little at the moment of contact. After that it is mostly balance and timing, the same as any stall.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort with the basic foot delays and a clean, vertical set are the foundation; the knee is just another stalling surface, so the cushioning instinct transfers. Bending the support leg to compensate for the raised stalling leg keeps you steady.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A head delay balances the bag on the top of the head. Set the bag just a few inches above your head, get directly under it, and look slightly upward so the top of the head is flat. As the bag lands, bend a little at the knees to cushion it so it settles rather than bouncing off.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Do not set too high; the higher the bag goes, the harder it lands and the harder it is to keep. A low set, about forehead height, that comes straight back down is much easier to receive. Get fully under the bag and sink slightly as it arrives, the same cushioning every stall needs.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: There is little to have first beyond a controlled, vertical set; many learn this early as one of the first non-foot stalls. The whole move is judging a low set and getting directly underneath it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A forehead delay balances the bag on the forehead. Set the bag straight up so it comes down a few inches above your head, look up to track it, and as it descends bend a little at the knees to cushion the landing and hold it in place. A softer, looser bag sits more easily on the forehead than a hard one.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It comes down to gentleness: cushion the bag's fall and let it settle rather than meeting it. Kick it high enough to get under it and plan the catch, look straight up as it descends, and give a little at the knees on impact. Many players find the forehead easier than other stalls because the dip is simple to judge.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It is worth having the basic delays, the toe, inside, and outside, before this one, since the cushioning instinct is the same and transfers directly. A controlled set that drops straight back down over your head is the main thing the move needs.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A neck delay balances the bag on the back of the neck. Set the bag in front of you a little above head height, then bend forward at the waist with your arms out and your shoulder blades raised, keeping your head up and your chin from tucking, so the back of the neck forms a flat shelf for the bag to land on.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The biggest mistake is tucking the chin; keep the head up and look forward so the neck stays flat. Bend at the waist without slouching, shoulder blades up, and position the neck under the bag as it comes down. The set height is forgiving, so this is one of the easier stalls once the posture clicks.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: There is little to have first beyond a controlled set that drops the bag just over your head; the move is mostly about posture, keeping the head up and the shoulder blades raised.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A shoulder delay balances the bag on the shoulder. Set the bag up to about head height and slightly back, then turn a little and lean down and to the side so the shoulder comes under the bag and cushions its fall. Leaning back slightly helps the bag settle into the pocket between the shoulder and neck.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Cushion the descent as with any delay, sinking and leaning to bring the shoulder under the bag rather than reaching up for it. Setting the bag slightly behind you and turning to meet it is what lets it rest on the shoulder. Using the cheek to help trap the bag is a useful crutch at first, though many players drop it once the placement feels natural.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A controlled set placed slightly behind you is the main thing the move needs; the rest is leaning and turning to bring the shoulder under the bag. Some flexibility helps for the lean.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A cloud delay balances the bag on the back of the shin, just below the knee. Catch it low rather than letting it fall from height, and bend the leg so the back of the shin forms a shelf; a small jump just before the catch gives the calf more give and makes it easier. How much the leg bends to hold the bag varies from player to player.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Catch the bag low; a bag dropping from a great height is hard to settle on the shin. A slight jump into the stall softens the landing on the calf. Loose, baggy trousers make this one much harder, since the fabric interferes with the surface. Balance and timing are the whole move once the leg position is found.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: This builds on a few other moves: a cloud kick to learn the surface, plus outside and sole stalls for the balance and backward-leg awareness it needs. It is one of the trickier basic delays, so it is worth having those first.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A peak delay balances the bag on the brim of a cap, so it needs a hat to perform. Flick the bag up to around eye or just-above-head level, then duck the head forward and down so the bag lands flat on the brim. A small upward tilt of the head as it lands keeps the brim level under the bag.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keep the set low, just a couple of inches above the brim, and slide the brim under the bag rather than waiting for it to drop from height. Looking slightly upward as the bag lands sets the head angle that holds it, and a poor head angle is the usual reason it slips. It can be dismounted with a quick flick of the head.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The only real requirement is a cap with a firm brim; the move is mostly about a low, controlled set and ducking the head under it. The cushioning instinct from the foot delays helps, but little else is needed.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The around-the-world kick circles the foot once around the bag between two toe kicks, rather than between delays. Kick the bag up, then circle the leg around it by lifting the knee, matching the leg's speed to the bag as it rises and falls, and kick it again. Keep the circle tight, just up and then down, so the top of it feels like an ordinary toe kick.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Lift the knee to make the circle rather than swinging the whole calf out to the side. Match the leg's pace to the bag, circling at the same rate it falls, and keep the loop small. It is a relatively simple, unhurried move where timing and balance matter more than speed, and one smooth pass is the goal. Treat it as a neat, self-contained supplemental move rather than a foundation for harder tricks.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Reliable toe kicks are the only real prerequisite; the kick version asks for the same precise toe contact with a circle added. Players comfortable with the around-the-world delay will find this a small step sideways.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A sole kick strikes the bag with the sole of the foot, usually with the kicking leg brought up behind the back. Bring the sole up high and flat behind you, bend the support leg for balance, and meet the bag with the sole; a flat sole sends it straight up, while an angled one sends it that direction. It can also be done straight out in front to push the bag to another player.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Flexibility and balance are what the move asks for. Practice holding the sole up flat behind you, bending the support leg to stay steady, and stretching gently if you need to get the sole level. Keep your eyes on the bag and read where it will land, since a flat sole controls the direction. It is closely related to an inside kick, just with the ankle bent further.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Enough flexibility to bring the sole up flat behind the back is the main requirement, along with steady one-footed balance. The sole delay shares the same backward-foot position, so comfort with one helps the other.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A spin is a full body rotation performed between two kicks or delays. Kick the bag up to about head height or higher, then turn a full circle, leading the spin with your head and whipping it quickly around behind your shoulder so you find the bag again with your eyes as soon as you come around, and kick or catch it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Lead with the head and spot the bag the way dancers spin: turn the head fast and find the bag the instant you come around, rather than letting the whole body drift around evenly. Set the bag high enough to give yourself time to complete the turn. Spins come naturally to some players and take real practice for others, so it is worth drilling the head turn on its own.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Solid basic kicks come first, since the spin is added between them and you need a dependable kick to recover on. The new skill is the spin itself and the spotting, keeping eye contact with the bag through the turn.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A butterfly kick is a leg over taken straight into a flying inside kick, all in the air. From a toe delay, set the bag straight up and slightly toward yourself, hop off the setting leg and pass the other leg over the bag, then strike the bag with the inside surface of the support foot while airborne and land on the leg that passed over. Aim to make contact at the apex of your jump.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Timing the contact to the top of the jump is the key; strike the bag at the apex while you are still rising into the move rather than on the way down. Set the bag slightly toward yourself so it stays in reach as you leave the ground. It is essentially the airborne, kicking cousin of the butterfly, so the over-the-top leg pass will feel familiar.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort passing a leg over the bag, as in a leg over or butterfly, plus a clean flying inside kick are the pieces this combines. Being able to act in the air, setting, passing the leg, and kicking before landing, is the demand the move adds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A cloud kick strikes the bag with the calf, the back of the shin. Bend the knee of the kicking leg and meet the bag when the calf is roughly horizontal to the ground, flexing the calf into the contact to drive the bag up. Pointing the toes and flexing the ankle keeps the kick controlled and the set straight.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Contacting the bag with the calf held horizontal gives the most control. Pointing the toes and flexing the ankle steadies the surface and keeps the bag going straight up rather than off to a side. Flexing the calf into the strike adds height to the kick.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It is a styling kick rather than a foundation, so there is little strictly required first; comfort bending the kicking leg deeply and judging the bag onto the calf is the main thing. It is the kicking counterpart of the cloud delay, which lands on the same surface.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A flying clipper is an airborne inside-clipper kick: you jump and strike the bag with the inside surface of the kicking foot while it is tucked across and behind the support leg, landing on the same foot that kicks. Set the bag high enough to get under it, jump off the kicking leg while swinging the other leg up and out of the way, bring the kicking foot into the cross-body position under the support knee, and meet the bag at the top of your jump.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Do not jump and kick at the same instant; jump first, then kick at the apex. The cleanest way in is to keep the non-kicking leg permanently up and out of the way, so you jump, kick, and land all on the one leg. Swinging the support leg out in front as you rise gives the kicking foot more room and stops the two legs from colliding, a common early problem. Keep the inside of the kicking foot from sitting too close to the support leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable inside kick and the balance to act in the air are the foundations; the move is essentially that kick performed off a jump. Comfort tucking the foot into the cross-body clipper position helps, since the strike happens there.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A squeeze catches the bag in the crook of the knee, the bend behind the joint. Set the bag about waist high, pivot on the support leg and bring the catching leg up with the thigh angled back rather than vertical, line the back of the knee up under the bag, and let it fall into the crook before squeezing the knee closed on it. Bend the support leg for balance.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Let the bag fall into the crook rather than chasing or grabbing it out of the air; lining the knee up under it and waiting is the whole move. Angle the upper leg back about forty-five degrees so the crook faces up, and bend the support leg to stay balanced. Setting from a toe delay with a small body pivot, or from beside the body off an outside delay, both make it easier to place the knee under the bag. It can be released by straightening the leg quickly to launch the bag back up.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Steady balance on one leg and a controlled set are the main requirements, since the move is about placement and timing rather than power. There is no kick or dexterity to learn first; the new skill is reading the bag into the crook and squeezing at the right moment.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A hop over holds an inside delay low to the ground, then hops the support leg up and over the delaying foot while the bag stays balanced on it. Set into a low inside delay, dip slightly on the support leg, then hop it up and over the ankle of the delaying foot, keeping that delay as still and stable as possible, and land. Leaning forward and lifting the arms helps you float the leg over.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keep the delaying foot low to the ground and as motionless as you can; the move is hopping over the bag, so moving the delay leg only makes it harder. Lifting the arms as you hop takes the strain off the leg and helps you float over. Hop over the ankle region, not up near the knee, and lean forward to gain momentum. Practicing the hop over a small gap on the ground can build the feel.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A stable, low inside delay you can hold while balancing is the core requirement, since nearly all your weight rests on the delaying leg through the hop. It is closely related to the eclipse, essentially the same idea performed on the ground rather than over a floating delay.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A ripwalk is a butterfly preceded by a stepping set: from a clipper, the setting foot passes under the support leg to set the bag, then you go straight into a butterfly. Set from the clipper under the support leg to about waist height, keeping the setting foot low and planting it immediately, then step the other leg over the bag and catch on a cross-body clipper. The set should let the bag peak just after the first dexterity, so move into the butterfly right away rather than waiting.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The stepping set is the heart of the move; keep the setting foot as low to the ground as possible and plant it fast, then bring the other leg over immediately rather than trying to jump over a high bag. Stay over the bag, since leaning back makes the closing butterfly much harder. Stringing together far butterflies (clipper to same-out to op-clipper) builds the speed and flipside control the ripwalk needs, and a clipper-set mirage trains the same set.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A fast, reliable butterfly and a clean stepping set are the two pieces this combines, so both should be comfortable first. Players often build to it through far butterflies and clipper-set mirages, which drill the set and the quick closing dexterity.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A double around the world circles the foot around the bag twice between two toe delays.
