Set the bag higher than you would for a single around the world to buy time, get the first circle in as quickly as possible, then rise onto the toes of the support foot for height and bring the leg around a second time before catching on the toe. Point the toes down through both circles.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Most of the speed is in the first circle; get it in immediately, then the second is more controlled. Set higher than a single and center the bag under the knee so the lower leg can circle without colliding with it. Rising onto the toes of the support foot gives the height for the second pass. Keep the toes pointed down, which cannot be overstated, so the bag does not snag or bounce off the foot the second time around.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Single around the worlds need to be thoroughly solid first, ideally automatic, since the double is two of them in quick succession. It is a stepping stone to a range of harder moves, so the time invested carries forward.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A torque is a clipper-set mirage taken straight into an osis. From a clipper, plant the setting foot and bring the other leg over the bag in an inside-to-outside dexterity, then, without needing to reset, spin toward the bag so it passes behind your back and catch it on the setting foot in a clipper, the osis catch. Plant the setting foot quickly and set a little to your side and in front of you.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Plant the setting foot as soon as the set is done; the height of the set decides how fast you then need to spin. Spin and spot the bag as you come around, using the momentum from the setting leg to carry you through the turn. Running through the mirage dexterity on its own first, checking how high and fast you can do it, is good preparation, since a strong dexterity makes the rest easier.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A torque combines a mirage, a spin, and an osis catch, so each of those should be comfortable on its own first. The spin and the timing of starting the osis as you come out of it are where most of the difficulty sits.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A blender begins like a whirl and turns into an osis. From a clipper set, bring the leg around the bag in a whirling motion but do not put it down; instead, as the leg comes around, turn so the bag passes behind your back and scoop it onto the setting foot in an osis. Set the bag a little higher and more behind you than for a whirl, around waist height, and keep it close to your body.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Genuinely commit to the whirl, then at the very last moment turn and finish as though you had been doing an osis all along. Begin the dexterity once the bag is a little above knee height, perform the whirl quickly, and keep the bag close to the body, since setting it too far out is the usual reason the osis catch misses. Keep your eye on the bag until the dexterity has started.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A whirl and an osis are the two halves of the move, so both should be solid first; comfort with a paradox whirl in particular carries over to the whirling entry. The new skill is committing to the whirl and converting it into the osis catch at the last instant.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A wrap holds an inside delay, then carries the bag around the support leg into a cross-body clipper without releasing it. Take an inside delay, keep the ankle cranked so the bag rests against the instep, then pull the foot around the support leg and into the clipper position, finishing by bringing the foot up again. Starting the inside delay high, dropping lower as you wrap, and rising sharply at the end gives a clean carry.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keep the ankle cranked through the wrap so the bag stays pinned against the instep rather than rolling off. The carry feels much like the swing of a pendulum, so players who have that move can borrow the same sensation. It also resembles a butterfly closely; many learn it by starting in a toe stall, flicking the bag behind and beside the body, bending the support leg, and slowly bringing the bag around the support leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dialed-in inside delay is the foundation, since the move begins and is carried on one. Comfort with the pendulum's carry, or with a butterfly, makes the wrapping motion click faster. It is the held-delay sibling of the hop over and walk over, which reach the same cross-body position by jumping or stepping instead of wrapping.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A cross-body sole stall catches the bag on the sole of the foot held across and behind the support leg, the cross-body cousin of the sole delay. Set the bag, drop into a cross-body position with the side of the kicking leg pressed against the support leg so the sole turns up and open, and bring the foot down with the bag to settle it on the flat sole. It takes more contortion than a clipper, so the cross-body position has to be set early, just before the bag arrives.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Get into the cross-body position early, well before the bag comes down, so the sole is already flat and facing up. Pulling the clipper foot in closer to the hip naturally rotates the sole upward. It is mostly flexibility: practice holding the bag on the sole and twisting the torso and leg in opposite directions until the cross-body position feels natural. Setting repeatedly from a same-side clipper teaches the feel of a flat, upside-down sole surface. A good drill is passing sole to flapper to flapper to sole across the body.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid sole delay is the foundation, plus the ankle flexibility to turn the sole up while the leg is crossed behind the body. Comfort with the clipper and flapper helps, since the cross-body sole is essentially their outside-surface relative; players who can already clip can often fall into it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A walk over holds an inside delay down on the ground, then steps the opposite leg over the delaying foot and into a clipper. Lower an inside stall to the ground without dropping the bag, plant your weight on the outside of that foot, then quickly step the other leg over and across into a clipper position. Keep the stalling foot straight and steady as you cross, and make the step over quick since most of your weight is bearing down on the top of that foot.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The first step over must be fast, because nearly all your weight presses down on the foot holding the bag. Keep that stalling foot straight and still while the other leg crosses. It works the ankle hard, so stretch well first; a useful builder is pressing the outside of the ankle against the floor while seated and slowly working it until the position is comfortable. Done quickly it is simple and stylish.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Being able to lower an inside stall to the ground and hold it while your weight rolls onto the outside of the foot is the core requirement; weak ankles struggle with it. Schooling inside delays and wraps builds the ankle flexibility the move depends on. It is the stepping sibling of the wrap and the hop over, which reach the same position by wrapping or hopping instead of stepping.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Rake is the reverse of a pendulum: you toe-delay the bag behind you, more to your side, then scoop it by swinging the leg forward and across the front of your body. Twist the body toward the bag as it comes down, catch it on the toe out to the side, and as it settles swing the leg forward, raking the bag across your front. It takes a strong body turn, around ninety degrees, to bring the leg through.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Many learn it by starting from a mirage and adding a small body spin during the dexterity, around forty-five degrees, then building toward a ninety-degree turn. Thinking of it as an outside stall placed behind you, then twisting and grabbing the bag forward, helps. It is a genuinely difficult move that needs a lot of practice; the pendulum's swinging carry is the closest familiar feel, in reverse.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dependable toe delay you can place behind and to the side of you is the foundation. The pendulum is the natural companion, since rake is its forward-swinging reverse; comfort with the pendulum's carry, and with the body turn of a symposium-style mirage, both transfer.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dragon is a cross-body outside delay, the outside-surface relative of the clipper: the bag rests on the outside of the foot held across and behind the support leg. Bring the foot up into an outside-delay position, then rotate the leg behind you and across the body, bending the support leg, and settle the bag on the outside surface. The catch in that crossed-behind position is the hard part.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Start from a plain outside delay, then push or rotate the leg behind the body into the cross-body position; it gives a strong stretch and teaches the placement. It is demanding on flexibility and the position feels unnatural at first, so it helps to hold the bag on the outside surface in the crossed position and simply stay there until the ankle learns it. Bending the support leg makes the reach manageable.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable outside delay is the starting point, since the dragon is that delay carried behind and across the body. It asks for real flexibility in the hip and ankle to hold the outside surface up while the leg is crossed behind the support leg; expect the position to take time to feel natural.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Eclipse is a hop over performed in the air. Jump, and immediately set the catching foot into an inside delay positioned out to the side, where a clipper would sit but in front of the support leg rather than behind it. Bring the jumping leg up and over the delaying leg while airborne, then come back down and finish in a clipper delay. Done well it looks like running on air.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Wait a little longer than feels natural before jumping, and jump off the opposite leg from the one catching the bag. The catching foot needs to be out to the side in a clipper-like position, just in front of the support leg instead of behind it. Many find the eclipse arrives almost on its own once the hop over is controlled enough to keep the bag steady through the move.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A controlled hop over is the direct prerequisite; eclipse is essentially the same move lifted off the ground, so being able to hold the bag through a hop over is what unlocks it. Comfort jumping and acting in the air, and a reliable inside-into-clipper catch, complete the picture.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A swirl is a cross-body around the world: from a clipper, the support leg circles the bag from behind, then up and over the top, back into a clipper delay. Set the bag low from the clipper, around eight inches up, pull it forward and arcing upward using the strength of the clipper leg, then continue circling the foot around and over the bag as it peaks, catching again on a same-side clipper. Keep the dex leg low rather than tucked up toward the body, which preserves the mobility to bring it around.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Set low; holding the dexterity leg high, close to the body, costs the mobility needed to circle the bag. Pull the bag forward and up out of the clipper using the clipper leg, then circle around it so you arrive at the top as the bag peaks rather than rushing it early. It is a small, controlled move, not a big fast one. Drilling plain clipper-set, single-dex cross-body around-the-worlds, without adding other operators, is the way in.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid clipper delay to set from and land on is essential, since the swirl begins and ends on one. Comfort with the around-the-world circling motion transfers directly; the swirl is that circle performed cross-body off a clipper rather than between toe delays.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Barfly sets from a clipper, then brings the setting leg around the bag from outside to inside twice, hopping off the support leg to finish on a cross-body clipper. Set the bag about waist height so it travels across your body from the setting side to the other, leave the setting foot unplanted for more time, and circle the leg around the bag twice as it descends, catching on a clipper. The bag should peak around groin height; begin the dexterities as it starts back down.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: What makes it a barfly is the cross-body set, not the clipper specifically; it can be set from any cross-body, a flying clipper or a butterfly as well. Turning the far hip toward the bag puts it closer to the dexing leg and gives a more natural position. Not planting the setting foot buys time for the two dexterities. Point the toe through the circles.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort circling a leg around the bag from a cross-body set is the core skill, so a reliable clipper set and a leg-over feel both help; many think of the barfly as a double-dex extension of that motion. The timing of starting the dexterities as the bag peaks and descends is the part that takes practice.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Paradon sets from a toe, then circles the bag twice from outside to inside with the opposite leg before catching on a cross-body clipper. Set the bag about waist height and slightly across your body so it falls toward the dexing foot, plant the setting foot immediately, hop hard off the support leg to get the first dexterity around, then carry the second into a clipper delay. The whole second half is essentially a butterfly catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The hard part is the timing of the first dexterity; hopping hard with the support leg is what gets it around in time, and after that it plays out like a butterfly. Set a bit cross-body so the bag is by the dexing foot as it descends, and plant the setting foot right after the set. Drilling the double around the world and the double switchover builds the two-circle motion the paradon needs.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable double around the world and a butterfly are the building blocks, since paradon combines the two-circle dexterity with a butterfly-style clipper catch. Comfort hopping firmly off the support leg to drive the first dex is the timing skill the move adds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Pixie mirage runs a pixie set straight into a mirage. From a toe, do a clean pixie set, circling the foot under and around the bag so it travels straight up rather than shifting to the side, then complete a mirage dexterity and catch on the setting toe. Treat it as a mirage with a pixie folded into the set, performed smoothly without rushing either half.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The pixie set is the key; lean over the bag and set it vertically, right under your knee, turning the hip and knee outward so the bag goes straight up and down. If the set is clean the mirage that follows is straightforward. Twist at the hips to finish the mirage dexterity. Keep the whole move unhurried, since rushing the pixie set shifts the bag off line.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean, controlled pixie set and a reliable mirage are the two halves, so both should be comfortable first. The move is mostly about getting a good-height pixie set that sends the bag straight up, after which the mirage finish feels familiar.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A flying inside is an airborne inside kick: jump and strike the bag with the inside surface of the foot while in the air. Kick the bag up high enough to give yourself time, jump off the support leg with the kicking leg angled down toward the ground, then kick the bag with the inside of the foot as it descends, landing on one leg and then the other. Jump with the same foot you are about to kick with.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Hit the bag high enough to have time to leave the ground before the kick. Jump off the kicking leg, meet the bag with the inside surface while airborne, and land softly one foot at a time. Though it looks trivial, it is the move that teaches symposium, the feel of acting in the air without planting, which carries into the jester, butterfly, and other flying moves.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A basic inside kick and the willingness to leave the ground are all it really needs; it is one of the first flying moves a player learns. Its value is as a stepping stone, building the airborne timing the harder flyers depend on.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A double pixie circles the foot around the bag twice from inside to outside, between a toe set and an opposite toe delay. From a toe, do a pixie set, plant the setting foot, then circle the foot around the bag a second time with a small hop and catch on the opposite toe. It is two pixie circles in succession off one set.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Get the pixie set solid first, since the move is built on it. After the set, plant the foot, then make the second circle small and add a small hop to clear the bag and reach the opposite-toe catch. Pointing the toes down through both circles, as in a double around the world, keeps the bag from snagging.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean, repeatable pixie set is the prerequisite, and a double around the world shares the two-circle motion. Once a single pixie is automatic, the double is mostly adding the second circle and the hop to the opposite-toe catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Flail is a symposium illusion: an illusion performed without planting the setting foot, so the body stays airborne through the dexterity. From a toe, set just under waist height, leave the setting foot unplanted, and circle the leg around the bag from outside to inside with the ankle while in the air, catching on the setting toe just as the dexterity leg lands.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Do not set too high; a set just under waist height keeps the timing manageable. Circle with the ankle, not the whole leg, and catch the ending delay right as the dex leg is coming down. The symposium part, staying up without planting, is helped by using the abdominal muscles to sit up in the air and bring the legs higher. A small half-inch hop just before the move loads the legs like a spring for the lift.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid illusion is the base, since flail is that move performed airborne without a plant. The new skill is symposium height, getting and holding the body up off a single set; players often build it with a small pre-hop and core engagement before this and other no-plant moves.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Dimwalk runs a pixie set straight into a butterfly. From a toe, pixie set the bag about waist height and slightly across your body, plant the setting foot as soon as you can, then perform a butterfly as the bag descends, finishing on a cross-body clipper. It can be done with or without planting; planting keeps the two dexterities cleaner, while skipping the plant is easier but slurs them together.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Solid pixie sets are the foundation; set about waist height and a bit cross-body, then plant quickly and let the second half play out as an ordinary butterfly. Planting the setting foot keeps the dexes distinct; not planting is easier but blends them. Following the bag with your eyes through the move helps a lot.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean pixie set and a reliable butterfly are the two pieces this combines. Once the pixie set sends the bag up cleanly, the move is essentially a butterfly off it; it is also good practice toward other pixie-led compounds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Blur runs a stepping set into a paradox mirage. From a clipper, do a low stepping set keeping the clipper foot close to the support leg, then go straight into a paradox mirage, hopping off the setting leg to bring the other leg around the bag from inside to outside and catching on the opposite toe. Set about waist height with a quick motion and keep the setting foot low to the ground.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Two things make a blur: a clean stepping set and a solid paradox mirage. Keep the clipper close to the support leg on the initial stall, set quickly to about waist height, and keep the stepping foot low. Plant the stepping leg as soon as it finishes the first dexterity, and the moment it touches down bring the original leg up for the mirage. The timing of that fast plant is the whole move.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable stepping set and a paradox mirage are the two halves, so both should be comfortable on their own first. The new skill is the quick plant between them, getting the stepping foot down fast enough to launch the mirage in time.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Miraging eclipse runs a mirage straight into an eclipse. From a clipper, do an in-to-out mirage dexterity, then jump and immediately set an inside delay out to the side, bring the jumping leg over the delaying leg while airborne, and come down into a clipper. It is a mirage that lands not on a stall but into the airborne hop-over of an eclipse.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The eclipse is the hard part, as in any eclipse: wait a beat before jumping and make contact with the bag at the last moment. The mirage entry changes the timing and often throws people off, so set at about waist height, keep your eye on the bag, then jump and finish. A good build is to practice a miraging inside stall into an eclipse or hop over separately, then blend them into one move.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid mirage and a controlled eclipse are the building blocks; comfort with the eclipse's airborne hop-over matters most, since the mirage just feeds into it. Practicing the mirage-into-inside-stall and the eclipse as separate pieces first makes joining them easier.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Double pickup is an inside-to-outside double around the world set from a clipper instead of between toe delays. From a clipper, set the bag just above waist height, quickly plant the setting leg, then circle the foot around the bag twice from inside to outside, landing on the opposite toe. It is the around-the-world circling motion doubled, off a clipper entry.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Plant the setting foot quickly after the set, or there isn't time to start the two dexterities; the motion is much like a barrage. Do not set too high, which makes the timing harder. Once the leg is around in front of the bag, keep it moving through the second circle. It is essentially a double around the world with a clipper set.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid double around the world is the direct prerequisite, since double pickup is that move set from a clipper. Comfort setting and planting quickly off a clipper, as in a barrage, is the part that differs from the toe-set version.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Butterfly swirl is a butterfly that finishes with a swirl instead of a plain clipper. Do the butterfly's over-the-top leg pass, then rather than simply catching the clipper, carry straight into a cross-body swirl, circling the foot around and over the bag before settling on the clipper delay. Stretching the swirling leg out a little in the butterfly position turns the torso and sets up the swirl.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: This move largely arrives once the swirl is solid; many hit it the same day they get the swirl. Curve the body and reach the swirling leg out a bit in the butterfly position, which rotates the torso into the swirl. Drilling clipper-set and especially toe-set swirls builds the foundation, and the more you hop over the bag, the easier the move becomes.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable swirl is the key prerequisite, and a butterfly is the other half; the move is the two strung together. Toe-set swirls in particular transfer directly, so getting those comfortable first makes the butterfly swirl straightforward.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Double switch over is a double-dexterity leg over: from a toe set, the support leg circles the bag twice from outside to inside before catching on the opposite toe. Hop off the support leg right as you set, bring it around the bag twice, and follow through into the toe delay. It is a double around the world performed with the leg passing over, out to in.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Do the first dexterity at the same time as the set, which makes it far easier. The second circle often feels like it won't make it, so commit and follow through; early attempts often land an inch off the ground. Jumping with the non-setting leg right as you set buys the time for both circles. If you have the double around the world, this is the same motion.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid double around the world is the natural lead-in, since the circling motion is the same; players usually learn this right after it. A reliable toe set and the timing to hop as you set complete the requirements.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Pixie same clipper sets from a toe, does a quick inside-to-outside dexterity with the setting leg, and catches on a same-foot clipper. From the toe set, circle the setting foot around the bag, then hop and twist your body toward that side to bring the foot into the cross-body clipper position, catching on the same foot you set with. Set around knee height.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The hop after the dexterity is the key; twist the body toward the side you hop to so the foot arrives in the clipper position. Set around knee height and aim for one fluid motion, though a slight hesitation helps at first. Jump a little and twist into the catch, bending the knee as you land to settle the bag. The move also helps build cleaner pixie sets.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A controlled toe set, the inside-to-outside dexterity of a pixie, and a reliable clipper catch are the pieces this combines. Comfort hopping and twisting into a same-side clipper is the timing skill it adds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Ducking butterfly threads a duck under the bag before a butterfly. Set the bag, ideally from a toe with a slight arc, and as it goes up duck your head and upper body under it, moving from one side to the other, then perform a butterfly, passing the leg over the top and catching on a cross-body clipper. Starting the duck the moment you set keeps the timing together.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Put a slight arc on the set so there is room to duck underneath, and begin the duck as soon as you set, moving the head across from one side to the other. With the legs braced a little apart and the upper body already ducked, you finish set up for the butterfly. From there it is an ordinary butterfly over the top.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable butterfly is the foundation, since the move adds a duck in front of it. Comfort ducking the head and torso under a set bag is the new element; many find a toe set with a small arc the easiest way to make room for the duck.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Symposium reverse whirl is a no-plant whirl that circles the bag the opposite way to a standard whirl. From a clipper, jump hard off the support leg and bring it over the bag from behind, up and over, without ever planting the other leg, landing back on the leg you jumped from. As in any whirl, the dexterity comes from the knee, not the hip.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The reverse-whirl motion comes from pointing the knee inward and drawing the dexing leg's heel toward the calf; those two together create the whirling circle in the other direction. Keep the dexterity in the knee rather than the hip, which would turn it into a butterfly-like move. The symposium part, completing it all without planting, is the demanding element.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Strong symposium ability is the foundation; being able to hold the body up through a no-plant move like the symposium whirl carries directly over. A solid reverse whirl supplies the circling direction, so comfort with both is what the move combines.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Paradox whirl is a whirl set from one clipper and caught on the other, with the setting leg doing the whirl. Set the bag so it travels across the front of your body, bring the setting leg from behind the support leg and, without planting it, carry it into the whirl motion, finishing on a clipper caught with the other leg. Set fairly low, around thigh level.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The move is in the set: a low, controlled set across the body sets up everything. Perform a quick whirl dexterity with the setting leg, then catch the clipper. Setting around thigh height keeps the timing tight enough to bring the leg around without planting.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable whirl and a clipper delay on both sides are the foundation, since paradox whirl sets from one clipper and lands on the other. The new element is carrying the setting leg straight into the whirl from behind the support leg without putting it down.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Spinning paradox symposium whirl adds a body spin to the front of a paradox symposium whirl. Set the bag high, around chest or head height, spin, spotting the bag as fast as you can, then come out of the spin into the no-plant paradox whirl, carrying the leg over the bag and catching on a cross-body clipper. Take the magic hop either during the spin or as you come around out of it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Set high while learning, especially if the spin isn't yet fast, so there is time to complete the turn and still make the whirl. Spot the bag the instant you can. The hop can come during the spin or as you come around, spinning roughly halfway, spotting, then hopping into the dexterity; find which timing suits you.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean paradox symposium whirl and a controlled spin are the building blocks, since this adds the spin to the front of that move. Spinning while keeping eye contact with the bag, and recovering into a no-plant whirl, are the combined skills it asks for.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Ducking paradox symposium whirl threads a duck into a paradox symposium whirl. Set the bag as you would for a tomahawk, duck under it as it rises, plant the setting foot and raise your original support leg, then carry into the no-plant paradox whirl and catch on a cross-body clipper. The duck happens at the front, before the whirl dexterity.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Have the paradox symposium whirl and the tomahawk solid first; once those are comfortable this is a manageable step up. Set as for a tomahawk, and time the duck so that as you pass under the bag you plant the setting foot and bring the original leg up, arriving set up for the whirl.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dialed paradox symposium whirl and a tomahawk are the stated prerequisites; the move adds a duck to that base. Comfort ducking the head and body under the bag while managing the no-plant whirl timing is the new combination.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Spinning paradox torque puts a body spin in front of a paradox torque, so the move carries two spins, the added one and the torque's own. From a clipper, spin, and rather than completing a full turn before the dexterity, spin about three-quarters around and then dex, carrying into the paradox mirage and the torque's spin to catch on an osis-style clipper. Keep everything close to the body.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The awkward part is that the spin direction changes between the two spins. You do not need the full turn before dexing; spinning roughly two hundred seventy degrees and then dexing works better. Keep the torque's dexterity twisting and close to the body, since scooping the osis catch cleanly is very hard if the bag drifts out.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid torque and a controlled spin are the foundation, plus comfort with paradox on the dexterity. The genuinely new difficulty is managing two spins in changing directions and still scooping the osis catch close to the body.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Paradox double leg over is a paradox mirage with an extra leg over added at the end. From a clipper, do an inside-to-outside paradox dexterity with the setting leg, then bring the other leg around from outside to inside, catching on the toe. Set about waist to belly height right in front of the support leg, and jump as the bag peaks so you land just as you delay.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: As with any paradox move, turning the hips helps you get the first dexterity around faster. Set in front of the support leg at waist to belly height, and time the jump to the bag's highest point so you come down exactly as you catch. Once the set and the paradox hip movement are reliable, the closing leg over follows.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A paradox mirage and a single leg over are the two pieces this combines, so both should be comfortable first. The paradox hip turn that speeds the first dexterity is the technique most worth having down before adding the second leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Gyro symposium torque stacks a gyro spin onto a symposium torque, giving the move several body events. Spin quickly, and as you come around plant the setting foot, spot the bag, then jump hard off that leg so it passes over the bag, using your momentum and arms, and catch on an osis-style clipper. Momentum carried out of the spin is what powers the dexterity.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Use the spin's momentum rather than fighting it: spin fast, plant the setting foot as you come around, and let that momentum, helped by swinging the arms, drive the jump that carries the leg over the bag. Spot the bag the moment you come around so you can time the osis catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A symposium torque is the base, with a gyro spin added in front; comfort spinning into a no-plant torque is the core requirement. Converting spin momentum into the over-the-bag jump and landing the osis catch is the combined skill the move develops.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Blurriest is a barfly with a stepping set in front of it. From a clipper, plant the setting foot and bring the other leg over the bag from inside to outside, then quickly plant that leg, hop off the first foot and circle it around the bag from outside to inside twice, finishing on a cross-body clipper. The opening leg-over is the stepping set; everything after it is an ordinary barfly.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A strong stepping set is the key asset. If both your stepping set and your barfly are solid, the move is less hard than it looks: step-set the bag to about waist height, then follow through exactly as you would for a barfly. The quick plant between the stepping set and the barfly circles is the timing that has to be clean.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable stepping set and a solid barfly are the two halves; the move is a barfly entered through a stepping set. Comfort with the barfly's double out-to-in circle is what most of it rests on.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Nemesis circles the bag twice with one leg and then twice with the other, from a clipper set to a cross-body clipper. From a clipper, circle the bag from inside to outside twice with the setting leg, plant, then circle twice more with the support leg from outside to inside, catching on a clipper. It is two double-dexterities back to back, so the set needs to be high enough to buy time for four circles.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Set the bag high, since four circles need time. Quick planting after the first leg's two dexes is essential to get the second leg started in time. It builds directly on the barfly, which supplies the double-circle motion; once a barfly feels routine off a high set, extending to the second leg is the next step.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid barfly is the foundation, since nemesis is essentially a barfly with a second double-dexterity added. The ability to set high and keep the four circles clean, with a fast plant between the two legs, is what the move demands.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Barrage is a double-dexterity mirage that runs from a clipper set to a toe stall. Set the bag from a clipper, immediately plant the setting leg, then bring the support leg over the bag from inside to outside twice, landing on a toe delay with the original setting foot. Keep the set low, since a high set makes the two circles harder to time.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Do not set too high; a lower set makes the move easier to learn. Plant the setting leg as fast as you can after the set, the faster the better, then start circling the support leg immediately. The two quick inside-to-outside circles into the toe catch are the whole move.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clipper set to launch from and a reliable toe delay to catch on are the basics, plus the double-circle motion shared with the double around the world. Quick planting off the clipper set is the timing skill barrage trains, which carries into many faster compounds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Ricochet circles the bag twice and catches it on a flapper, a sole delay held across the body. From a toe set, hop off the support leg and circle the bag twice from outside to inside, then settle it onto the sole on the far side. It pairs a double dexterity with the difficult cross-body sole catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: This is a hard move that combines a double circle with a flapper catch. Getting the bag up high and circling quickly buys time to wrap the leg around and into the sole position. The cross-body sole catch is the limiting skill; until it is dependable on its own, the double-dex entry has nothing to land on.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A double around the world supplies the two-circle motion, and a flapper, the cross-body sole delay, supplies the catch; both need to be solid, since ricochet joins them. The sole catch after a fast double dexterity is the genuinely demanding part.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Pixie opposite clipper sets from a toe, circles the setting foot around the bag from inside to outside, and catches on the opposite-foot clipper. From the toe set, do a quick pixie-style circle with the setting leg, then bring the bag across to a cross-body clipper caught on the other foot.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The dexterity can feel awkward at first. A useful drill is doing a normal pixie barefoot, circling the foot as fast as you can and then simply stopping and planting, to get the feel of the quick circle. Once that motion is comfortable, redirect the catch to the opposite clipper.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean pixie circle off a toe set is the core skill, plus a reliable clipper delay on the opposite foot. It is a close cousin of the same-foot version, differing only in which foot makes the clipper catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Fairy mirage runs a fairy set into a mirage. From a toe, do a fairy set, leaning over the bag and lifting it vertically toward the knee, then once it leaves the foot turn the hip and knee to the inside of the body to send it straight up, and complete a mirage to catch on the setting toe. It is a mirage entered through a fairy set rather than a plain toe set.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The fairy set is the key, and it helps to have the pixie set first, since the technique is similar though the fairy is a touch harder. Lean over the bag and set it vertically, lifting toward the knee, then turn the hip and knee inward once the bag has left the foot so it travels straight up. A clean, vertical set makes the mirage finish straightforward.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A controlled fairy set and a reliable mirage are the two halves; a solid pixie set is good preparation, since the fairy set is its close relative. The move is mostly about getting the fairy set to send the bag straight up before the mirage.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Probe is a clipper delay set from the opposite shoulder, so the bag passes behind your head, out of sight, on its way to the foot. Hold the bag on the shoulder, throw it up and across with the shoulder so it travels behind your head, then bring the foot into the cross-body clipper position and catch it blind as it comes down on the far side.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Master the shoulder delay and the clipper separately first; once both are reliable the move is fairly approachable. The trick is learning to throw the bag with the shoulder and trust the blind catch, since the bag passes behind your head; delay it on the shoulder, pop it across, then simply clip it on the way down.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dependable shoulder delay and a clipper are the two prerequisites, since probe links them with a blind, behind-the-head toss. Comfort catching a clipper without watching the bag the whole way is the new element it adds.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Double over down circles the bag around the setting foot twice on the way down and finishes on a clipper. From a toe stall, settle your balance, then begin circling the bag as it rises; treat the first circle like a butterfly dex and the second like a reverse whirl, sending the bag in front of the foot rather than behind it. As the foot clears the bag at the top of the second circle, relax and bring it down into the clipper catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The move rewards agility more than power. A good preparation is an out-in double around the world, which travels around the bag twice; once that is dialed, double over down asks for far less circling. Drilling the circular leg motion on its own, even barefoot without a bag, builds the speed the dexes need.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Double over down is the foundation for the wider over-down and double-down family, so it is worth getting solid before building on it. A reliable toe stall and clipper are the catches, and comfort with the around-the-world circling motion is the skill it leans on most.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Down double down sets off a down and then circles the bag twice on the way down into a clipper. Set the bag about waist height and plant the setting foot as soon as possible, turning toward the bag so it travels almost between your legs and ends up in front of you, which opens room for the dexes. Keep the spin from getting too slow and start the dexterities quickly, dexing as deeply as you can.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Among the related over-down moves this is one of the harder ones, but a few adjustments make it reachable. Turning into the bag so it sits in front of you is the main key, and it is easy to hit unclean, so watch that each circle fully clears the foot. A dependable down-time double down underneath it makes everything else fall into place.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid double down with good downtime is the core requirement, since down double down adds a down entry in front of it. Waist-height setting and a quick, deep dex are the habits to bring; rushing the spin or setting too high are the usual stumbling points.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Atomic butterfly is a butterfly built on an atomic set, where the dex begins while the bag is still being set. Set straight up, around belt height and a short distance from the body, and start jumping as you set rather than after; by the time the setting foot plants you should already be coming over the bag. The path around the bag is diagonal, almost a quarter-ellipse, pulling the setting knee across toward the opposite leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Everything depends on the set. The hard part is starting the dexterity during the set instead of waiting for the bag to rise, so jump off the plant leg as you set and let the motion flow straight into the circle. Keeping the set close and vertical buys the time needed to get over the bag.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean, controlled atomic set is the prerequisite that makes or breaks the move, along with a reliable butterfly to finish into. Comfort jumping and turning the hips while the bag is still leaving the foot is the new timing it asks for.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Blurry whirl runs a blurry set into a whirl. Start from a solid clipper and set around waist to stomach height; the set does not need to be high. The key is to begin the paradox turn almost at the same moment as the first dex, rather than setting and then turning, which preserves time for the second dex. Jump off the support leg as you start the set, point the knee outward, and plant the setting foot as soon as possible.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Take your time on entry and lean on a solid clipper. The hardest piece is the stepping set, and folding the paradox turn into the first dex is what separates a clean blurry whirl from a rushed one. Having paradox whirls already dialed makes the whole move noticeably easier.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable clipper and a comfortable paradox whirl are the foundations, since blurry whirl combines the blurry set with that turning finish. The added challenge is the timing of the stepping set and starting the turn early, not raw set height.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Fairy pops the bag up and circles the setting leg around it, landing back without the support leg having to move. Pop the bag up cleanly so that by the time you have finished circling the leg, the support foot is still where it started and the bag drops straight back to it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It is mostly a timing move. A useful way to learn it is to pass the bag from one foot to the other and then hop over it, which builds the feel for when to bring the leg around. Once the timing clicks the circle becomes natural.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort hopping over the bag and basic foot-to-foot control are enough to start. A controlled upward pop that keeps the bag over the support foot is the main thing to get right.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Fog is best understood as a blurry double leg over: a blurry set leading into a double leg over, rather than a blur with a leg over added at the end. Set just above waist height without overdoing it, turn slightly toward the side you set from, and jump off your initial support leg. It helps to treat the move as two parts, the set first and then the double leg over.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keeping the set modest is important, since a set that is too big makes the double leg over rushed. The placement of the bag after the clipper delay matters a great deal, because it needs to sit in a comfortable position to get the leg over, and that comes with practice.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean blurry set and a solid double leg over are the two halves to bring together. Reading the move as blurry-into-double-leg-over, rather than as one long sequence, makes the timing much easier to find.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Magellan combines a pixie set with a leg over. Make a good pixie set around waist height with the toe pointed slightly upward, then bring the foot back to the ground, just touching the toes, and go straight into the leg over. Keeping the bag close to the body, almost underneath you, keeps both circles small and tight.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The hard part is timing the first dex: hesitate a split second before starting the pixie dex rather than rushing it. With the pixie set and leg over already learned, the rest is mostly speed. If the pixie set is giving trouble, drilling an around the world first helps build it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable pixie set and a leg over are the prerequisites, since magellan links them. A waist-height pixie set with a slightly upturned toe is the specific skill to have solid before putting the two together.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Spinning osis adds a spin to an osis. After catching the first clipper, spin a half turn to set up the osis, let the bag come down close to you, almost into your back pocket, and spin again as you take it, feeling it meet the foot before turning into the clipper delay that finishes the osis.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Build it in steps before joining them: get the clipper solid, then the spinning, then put the osis on top. Learning to feel the bag hit the foot, rather than watching it the whole way, is what lets the two spins flow into one continuous motion.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dependable clipper and comfort spinning are the foundations, since spinning osis is an osis wrapped in a spin. Placing the bag close as it comes down, almost behind the hip, is the positioning that makes the second spin and the clipper delay work.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Atom smasher runs an illusion straight into a mirage. Set a little higher than you would for an illusion, above waist height, and do the illusion; the moment the outside dex foot hits the ground, whip the other leg around in an S shape and mirage to finish. Keep the dexing leg bent at the knee and twist it quickly inward, down and around the bag, planting about half a foot out.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The link between the two halves is everything: the second leg has to start the instant the first dex lands. Setting a touch higher than a plain illusion buys the time to bring the mirage leg around cleanly.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean illusion and a reliable mirage are the two pieces; the move simply connects them back to back. The new skill is the fast S-shaped whip of the second leg straight out of the illusion.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Atomic illusion is an illusion off an atomic set. Make a quick, straight set parallel to the main axis of your body, as straight up and high as you would for an atom smasher, then plant the dexing leg fast and bust the cleanest, fastest illusion you can.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Being able to hit an atom smasher cleanly first is the strongest preparation, because it requires the same tall, straight set. Train the move on both sides, and start slower than full speed until the timing is reliable before pushing the pace.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid atom smasher and a fast, clean illusion are the foundations. The demand it adds is set quality: a tall, vertical atomic set that gives the illusion enough time to finish.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Barraging butterfly starts like a symposium whirl, then steps the bag under the leg again before finishing with a butterfly. Begin the dex as you would for a symposium whirl; after that dex, quickly move the leg so the bag passes under it once more, the way a stepping set does, and the butterfly at the end falls into place.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It is one of the more approachable moves of its kind once the entry is clean. The hard part is making the first two dexes serve as the set, so clear that opening cleanly and the butterfly becomes easy.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A comfortable symposium whirl entry and a stepping set are the building blocks, with a butterfly to finish. The challenge is chaining the whirl dex and the stepping motion together before the butterfly.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Bedwetter is closely related to fog but wants a noticeably higher set. Set the bag a good deal higher than you would for fog so there is room to complete the second dex cleanly.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The single biggest adjustment from fog is set height: too low and the second dex gets cramped and dies. Give the move room by setting tall.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort with fog is the natural starting point, since bedwetter shares its shape and differs mainly in set height. The new habit is trusting a taller set to make space for the second dex.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Double around the world heel circles the bag twice and catches it on the heel, which demands a lot of fine control. Keep the circling motion tight and controlled so the bag stays in position for the heel catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A helpful way in is to learn it with a larger, sand-filled bag of the softer kind that stalls with very little effort, which makes the heel catch far more forgiving while you build the control. Move to a normal bag once the motion is consistent.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable heel stall and comfort with around-the-world circling are the prerequisites. The added difficulty is the control needed to keep the bag placed for a heel catch after two full circles.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Ducking barfly is a barfly with a duck of the head, so your barfly needs to be solid first. Lift the dexing foot before the bag even comes down and start the dex as soon as possible; the move has to be fast to stay clean. A good set lets you duck without moving much, so you always know where the bag is and which ankle it is heading for.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Speed is the whole game: get the dexing foot up early and dex immediately. A well-placed set you can duck under without shifting your body is what makes that speed possible.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A dependable barfly is the core requirement, plus comfort ducking the head under the bag. The new element is the quickness, lifting and dexing before the bag arrives.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Ducking osis ducks the head under the bag and finishes on an osis. Set the bag around neck height and swing your head underneath so you can watch the bag drop beside you; once it falls toward your waist, bail into the osis rather than your normal clipper stall.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Drilling ducking butterfly alongside osis and far osis builds the pieces. A useful practice loop is alternating osis and ducking osis back to back so the duck and the catch start to feel like one motion.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable osis and comfort ducking the head under the bag are the two halves. The change from a plain osis is committing to the duck and reading the bag from beside your head.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Dyno sets low and circles the foot fully around the bag as it descends. Set the bag low, hop slightly off the ground right after the set, crank the ankle all the way around, and travel around the bag as it comes down.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Getting comfortable with whirl and reverse whirl first makes dyno far more approachable, since it draws on the same circling feel. After that it is mostly repetition: cranking the ankle to full and timing the circle to the bag's descent.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Whirl and reverse whirl are the recommended groundwork. The move's own demands are a low set and a full, fast ankle crank around the bag as it falls.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Eggbeater is an illusion into a leg over. Set around stomach level, plant quickly, and make the first dex with the leg twisted inward, much like a close outside kick, keeping it from landing too far out; plant a little outward and then perform an opposite leg over.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Keeping the first dex from drifting too far out is the key, since it sets up the leg over that follows. A dependable illusion underneath makes the whole move much easier.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clean illusion and an opposite leg over are the two components. The new piece is the inward-twisted first dex that plants close enough to flow into the leg over.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Fairy illusion runs a fairy set into an illusion. Lean over the bag and set it vertically, lifting toward the knee; once the bag has left the foot, turn the hip and knee to the inside of the body so the bag travels straight up, then complete the illusion. Separating the two parts of the set, set, wait, then turn the hips, is what keeps it vertical.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A good, fast fairy set is the heart of the move, and a quick setting motion lets you plant the foot sooner and set just as high. Having a pixie set already is good preparation, since the fairy set is its slightly harder relative.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A controlled fairy set and a reliable illusion are the two halves; a solid pixie set is useful preparation. The move is mostly about getting the fairy set to send the bag straight up before the illusion.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Flying outside is an outside kick done airborne. Stand on your kicking leg with the other raised, and when the bag descends to about waist level, jump up, do the outside kick in the air, and land again only on the kicking leg.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Making the kicking leg do all of the work, taking off and landing on it alone, builds the strength and timing that later symposium moves rely on. It is worth practicing as a strength drill even once you have it.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A solid outside kick is the only real prerequisite. The new demands are the jump and the single-leg landing, which is where the strength benefit comes from.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Guay is a leg over that finishes in an inside delay rather than a toe delay. Circle the leg over the bag as you would for a leg over, but catch and hold it on the inside of the foot instead of the toe.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: If your leg over is solid, the only new piece is redirecting the catch to an inside delay. Getting comfortable holding the bag on the inside of the foot is the skill to focus on.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A reliable leg over and a steady inside delay are the two halves. The move simply swaps the usual toe catch for an inside-foot catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Knee clipper catches the bag on the knee in a crossed-leg, clipper-like position. Just before you jump, bring one leg as far across the center of your body as you can so it is out of the way, then jump off the kicking leg and bring its knee up to meet the bag just as the knee becomes flat.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: The move is largely a matter of hip flexibility and getting the support leg out of the way. Practicing by crossing the legs at the thigh as far over as possible builds the range it needs.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Comfort with crossed-leg clipper positioning and good hip flexibility are the foundations. The new element is meeting the bag on a flattened knee rather than the foot.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Leg over flapper stall circles the leg over the bag and catches it in a flapper stall. Set the bag high and take the dex high as well, so there is room to bring the foot to a level surface for the flapper catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Height is the key: a high set and a high dex create the space the flapper needs to settle on a flat foot. Crowding the move low is the usual reason the flapper fails.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A leg over and a steady flapper stall are the two halves. The move's own demand is setting and dexing tall enough to land the flapper cleanly.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Paradox symposium whirl combines a paradox whirl entry with a symposium whirl. Set the bag as you would for a paradox whirl but a bit higher, to allow the time needed to get the dexing leg ready for the symposium whirl.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Doing symposium whirls set from the toe is a good way to build the leg strength this move asks for, and strong paradox whirls help as well. The extra set height is what gives you room to prepare the second dex.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A strong paradox whirl and a symposium whirl are the two components. The move leans heavily on leg strength, so building that through toe-set symposium whirls is worthwhile preparation.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: Pickup is a relaxed, low-difficulty move. From a clipper set, move the opposite leg around the bag from inside to outside and catch the bag on the toe of that same leg, the one that dexed.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: It works best with a lower set than you might expect, which makes it a great save after a bad set. The whole motion is a single small circle of the opposite leg, so keep it loose.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.

new_value: A clipper set and a toe delay are all it needs. The only new piece is the small inside-to-outside circle of the opposite leg before the catch.
source_note: Extracted from the legacy footbag.org move description and community tips into structured instructional prose.
